chunk_id: docling_book_en_国外数学教材-非数学专业教材-线性代数-Gilbert Strang—Linear Algebra and Its Applications 4e_0363_2
text: Thus Ay is positive definite. Its eigenvalues (not the same M!) must be positive. Its determinant is their product, so all upper left determinants are positive.

chunk_id: docling_book_en_国外数学教材-非数学专业教材-线性代数-Gilbert Strang—Linear Algebra and Its Applications 4e_0363_3
text: If condition Ill holds, so does condition IV: According to Section 4.4, the kth pivot dk is the ratio of detAk to detAk-1. If the determinants are all positive, so are the pivots.

chunk_id: docling_book_en_国外数学教材-非数学专业教材-线性代数-Gilbert Strang—Linear Algebra and Its Applications 4e_0363_4
text: If condition IV holds, so does condition I: We are given positive pivots, and must deduce that xTAx > 0. This is what we did in the 2 by 2 case, by completing the square. The pivots were the numbers outside the squares. To see how that happens for symmetric matrices of any size, we go back to elimination on a symmetric matrix: A = LDL"

chunk_id: docling_book_en_国外数学教材-非数学专业教材-线性代数-Gilbert Strang—Linear Algebra and Its Applications 4e_0363_12
text: It is beautiful that elimination and completing the square are actually the same. Elimination removes x1 from all later equations. Similarly, the first square accounts for all terms in x'Ax involving x1. The sum of squares has the pivots outside. The multipliers lij are inside! You can see the numbers — and - 3 inside the squares in the example.
